one_line_takeaway: KVW removes an LVLM's forget-set-specific knowledge by contrasting forget-set vs. retain-set MLP knowledge-vector activations and exponentially down-scaling the forget-implicated vectors in a chosen layer range, achieving oracle-level forgetting on CLEAR (e.g., Forget5 accuracy 0.00 matching Oracle) with retain performance preserved, using only forward passes and no gradients.
key_finding: On MLLMU-Bench (LLaVA-1.5-7B, Table 1), KVW's forget ROUGE most closely tracks the Oracle (retrain-from-scratch) baseline across most splits while keeping retain performance close to Oracle, e.g. Forget05/Test1: KVW forget=0.561 vs Oracle=0.556 vs Vanilla=0.650; Forget05/Test2: KVW=0.567 vs Oracle=0.567 vs Vanilla=0.611. Gradient-based baselines (GA, GD, KL, NPO) show high variance across splits and sometimes violate the retain constraint; the original MMU diverges to zero ROUGE on all splits at 7B scale, so the authors substitute a self-built LoRA variant MMU* which still fails to reduce forget accuracy sufficiently (e.g. Forget10/Test1 MMU* forget=0.654 vs Oracle=0.525). On CLEAR (Qwen2-VL-2B, Table 2), KVW reaches Forget=0.00 matching Oracle=0.00 on Forget5 (both Test1/Test2) while other baselines leave nonzero forget accuracy (e.g. GD=0.14/0.18, NPO=0.21/0.28, MMU=0.29/0.26); GA achieves 0.09/0.03 forget but catastrophically collapses retain performance to 0.00/0.00/0.00 under Forget10. On computational cost (Figure 3, CLEAR Forget05), KVW is reported as substantially lower in FLOPs, wall-clock runtime, and peak memory than all gradient-based and LoRA-based baselines and the retrain-from-scratch Oracle, with memory usage comparable to plain inference since no gradients are stored. Ablation (Table 3) shows both design choices are necessary: without 'ans only' (restricting coefficient computation to answer tokens), forget accuracy stays high (0.15/0.16) and unlearning is ineffective; without 'w/ retain' (the Cr contrast term), forget accuracy hits 0.00 but retain performance collapses to 0.00, showing the retain-contrast term is essential to avoid indiscriminate weakening. Hyperparameter sensitivity analysis (Figure 5, gamma; Figure 6, layer range) shows KVW has a broad feasible operating region and lower sensitivity to its hyperparameters than training-based methods are to their loss-balancing coefficient lambda.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is Knowledge Vector Weakening (KVW), a training-free, gradient-free machine-unlearning method for large vision-language models (LVLMs). KVW operates purely via forward propagation: it treats each row vector of an FFN/MLP value matrix as a 'knowledge vector' and its corresponding activation coefficient (from Geva et al.'s key-value memory view of FFNs) as a 'knowledge coefficient.' For a given forget set, KVW computes the average knowledge coefficient over answer tokens (Cf) and contrasts it with the average coefficient over the retain set (Cr) via a log-ratio, defined as the Forget Knowledge Accessor A = max(0, log(Cf/Cr)). Knowledge vectors with high A are then scaled down by a gate function g(A) = exp(-gamma*A), where gamma is a single tunable weakening-strength hyperparameter, applied only within a chosen range of MLP layers (start/end layer, also hyperparameters). No backpropagation, retraining, or additional model parameters (e.g., LoRA adapters) are introduced; the procedure runs once over the forget set per layer range.
threat_model: Not an adversarial/attacker threat model; this is a model-owner/deployer capability paper. The stated motivation is regulatory/ethical/privacy: LVLMs can memorize and expose private, copyrighted, or hazardous information (e.g., a person's identity tied to their photo), and the paper's stated goal is enabling model owners to selectively and efficiently remove that memorized influence without full retraining. The paper does briefly discuss an 'adversarial user' angle in Section 6.2: it observes that training-based unlearning baselines (GD, KL, NPO, MMU) merely suppress the exact ground-truth token but leave the underlying identity knowledge largely intact, so an adversarial user could still infer sensitive information via indirect cues/hallucinated-but-related outputs (e.g., outputting a different but still identity-revealing description). This is presented as a robustness/leakage observation about baselines, not as a formal threat model with an attacker capability specification, and no adaptive-attack or robustness-against-relearning evaluation is performed for KVW itself.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitation (Conclusion): KVW weakens knowledge vectors individually and does not explicitly model interactions among knowledge vectors or compositional knowledge representations; they suggest future work on structured weakening and lightweight post-weakening adaptation. Additional limitations evident from the paper's own results: (1) the original MMUnlearner baseline could not be run as published at 7B scale (it diverged/collapsed to zero ROUGE), forcing the authors to construct their own LoRA-restricted MMU* variant, which weakens the strength of that particular comparison; (2) on CLEAR Forget10, KVW's own retain accuracy drops somewhat (0.40-0.43 vs Vanilla 0.42, and RealF drops to 0.89 in one fold) even though it satisfies the paper's retain constraint threshold; (3) the method still requires two hyperparameters (gamma, and a start/end layer range) chosen via validation search, so it is not fully hyperparameter-free, though the paper argues the search is more robust/stable than for gradient-based baselines; (4) evaluation is limited to two identity/profile-style unlearning benchmarks (MLLMU-Bench, CLEAR) and two backbones (LLaVA-1.5-7B, Qwen2-VL-2B), so generalization to other unlearning tasks (e.g., hazardous-knowledge or copyright unlearning) or larger models is not empirically demonstrated; (5) the paper does not evaluate KVW's own robustness to relearning/extraction attacks, only observing (qualitatively, Section 6.2) that training-based baselines leave residual identity knowledge inferable via indirect cues -- KVW is not adversarially stress-tested to the same depth.

datasets_used: MLLMU-Bench (500 synthetic individual profiles + 153 public celebrity profiles, 14 QA pairs per profile; Forget/Retain/Real-world splits; LLaVA-1.5-7B backbone; ROUGE metric)
CLEAR (200 synthetic individuals, ~3,700 images, built on TOFU; Forget/Retain/RealFace/RealWorld splits; Qwen2-VL-2B backbone; accuracy metric)
quantitative_results: MLLMU-Bench Forget05/Test1: KVW forget=0.561, retain=0.587, celeb=0.397 vs Oracle forget=0.556, retain=0.619, celeb=0.412 vs Vanilla forget=0.650
MLLMU-Bench Forget05/Test2: KVW forget=0.567, retain=0.595, celeb=0.381 vs Oracle forget=0.567, retain=0.610, celeb=0.410
CLEAR Forget5/Test1: KVW forget=0.00, retain=0.48, RealF=0.93, RealW=0.55 vs Oracle forget=0.00, retain=0.54, RealF=0.95, RealW=0.57 vs Vanilla forget=0.41
CLEAR Forget5/Test2: KVW forget=0.00, retain=0.48, RealF=0.93, RealW=0.55; GD forget=0.18; NPO forget=0.28; MMU forget=0.26
CLEAR Forget10/Test1: GA collapses to forget=0.00, retain=0.00, RealF=0.00, RealW=0.00 (retain-constraint violation); KVW forget=0.01, retain=0.43, RealF=0.92, RealW=0.48

Ablation (Table 3, CLEAR Forget05): full KVW (ans-only + w/retain) forget1=0.00, forget2=0.00, retain=0.48; w/retain only (no ans-only) forget1/2=0.15/0.16, retain=0.27; ans-only only (no w/retain) forget1/2=0.00/0.00, retain=0.00
Rank sensitivity of LoRA baselines on CLEAR forget05 (motivation section): GD forget accuracy on Test2 ranges from 4% at rank 16 to 18% at a lower rank, a >4.5x gap
baselines_compared: Oracle (retrain from scratch excluding forget data)
KL - KL-constrained gradient ascent (Nguyen et al. 2020)
NPO - Negative Preference Optimization (Zhang et al. 2024)
MMU / MMU* - MMUnlearner (Huo et al. 2025); original MMU diverged at 7B scale so authors built a LoRA-restricted MMU* variant (Appendix D) for MLLMU-Bench experiments
